models.title: Kossola / Cudjo Lewis
models.subtitle: A named person on the Clotilda, with a rare but still incomplete chain
models.confidence: mixed: documented, reported, inferred, and unknown
models.summary: Kossola was captured in a Dahomey raid, moved through Dahomey's slaving system toward Ouidah, transported on the Clotilda to Mobile, assigned to James Meaher, and later helped found Africatown after emancipation. His exact birthplace, route details, and daily work sites are not all fixed by the same archival chain.

why_enslaved.label: Raid by the Kingdom of Dahomey

nodes.label: Bante region, Benin
nodes.note: Often identified as Kossola's home region, not a surveyed village coordinate.

nodes.label: Dahomey raid and inland movement
nodes.note: Captured with others in a raid associated with the Kingdom of Dahomey.

nodes.label: Ouidah barracoon / market
nodes.note: Held before embarkation at the principal Slave Coast port used by the Clotilda.

nodes.label: Clotilda: Ouidah to Mobile
nodes.note: SlaveVoyages records 110 embarked and 108 disembarked on voyage 36990.

nodes.label: James Meaher / Mobile area
nodes.note: Enslaved.org records Kossola as given by Timothy Meaher to James Meaher.

nodes.label: Africatown
nodes.note: After emancipation, Clotilda survivors founded Africatown near Mobile.

models.title: Slave Coast Probability Corridor
models.subtitle: A group-level model for unnamed captives from the Bight of Benin
models.confidence: probable corridor, not an individual biography
models.summary: For an unnamed captive from the Slave Coast, the defensible visualization is a probability corridor: hinterland capture through war, raiding, debt, judicial sale, or kidnapping; movement through inland brokers and coastal markets; confinement near Ouidah; Atlantic shipment; sale or forced distribution at a landing port; then labor in plantation, household, port, or industrial settings depending on destination.

nodes.label: Bight of Benin hinterland
nodes.note: Possible inland origin zone. Exact home is unknown for most unnamed captives.

nodes.label: Dahomey / regional broker network
nodes.note: Regional wars, raids, judicial slavery, pawning, kidnapping, and broker networks fed the coastal trade.

nodes.label: Ouidah / Whydah market
nodes.note: Ouidah was one of the most active slave-trading ports on the Slave Coast.

nodes.label: Atlantic crossing
nodes.note: Specific ship, mortality, duration, and conditions depend on the voyage record.

nodes.label: Cuba, Brazil, Caribbean, or Gulf ports
nodes.note: Late-era Bight of Benin voyages often landed in Cuba, Brazil, the Caribbean, or rarely the U.S. Gulf Coast.

nodes.label: Sugar, coffee, cotton, port, or household labor
nodes.note: Plantation and life-condition claims require a joined sale, census, residence, or estate record.

models.title: Bora
models.subtitle: A named liberated African record with a short, unusually legible route
models.confidence: documented ship and fate, inferred language group
models.summary: Bora appears in the SlaveVoyages PAST data as a named man aboard the NS de Regla. The record preserves age, height, ship, captain, embarkation at Little Bassa, disembarkation at Freetown, court condemnation, and the fate 'claimed by father'. It does not preserve his exact ancestral home or why he was enslaved.
